t12: 8. From Mini-STRC to Ultra-Mini — the truncation path
lede: The original Mini-STRC (700–1775) already fit in AAV. The question we asked next: how much further can we cut before the TMEM145 binding surface breaks? Answer: a lot. The C-terminal-only construct (1075–1775) preserves the binding pocket at sub-Å RMSD while unlocking 1,125 bp of additional AAV headroom — enough to upgrade the regulatory cassette from bare promoter to a full OHC-exclusive architecture.
step1_body: The original clinical candidate. Removes the unstructured N-terminus (1–699) and the disordered LRR linker. Fits in AAV with 1,472 bp spare. Validated across 20 AF3 jobs. But the spare budget only covers a minimal promoter — no posttranscriptional boost.
step2_title: Can we cut the LRR domain?
step2_body: The LRR (Leucine-Rich Repeat) domain sits between the disordered N-terminus and the binding pocket. Its function is undocumented. If it is a passive spacer, deleting it doubles the AAV budget. If it participates in binding or dimerisation, Ultra-Mini fails. The only way to find out: run the AF3 jobs.
step3_body: The LRR domain is a passive spacer. Ultra-Mini preserves the TMEM145 interface at sub-Å RMSD, reproduces GOLD-zone contacts in a full-length AF3 multimer run, and retains homodimer geometry at the deep ARM cluster 1579–1581. Clinical construct.
table_title: Full comparison (four candidates considered, one selected)
